text.text: /// but memory ordering is still needed for visibility of non-atomic data.
/// 1. **Acquire**: When you load with Acquire, it creates a barrier that prevents
///    any memory operations AFTER the load from being reordered BEFORE the load.
///    This ensures that if another core/critical-section wrote data before storing
///    the atomic, you'll see that data when you read the atomic.
/// 2. **Release**: When you store with Release, it creates a barrier that prevents
///    any memory operations BEFORE the store from being reordered AFTER the store.
///    This ensures that any data you wrote before updating the atomic will be
///    visible to other cores/critical-sections when they see the new atomic value.
/// 3. **Acquire + Release**: Together, they create a "critical section" memory
///    barrier that ensures operations inside the critical section stay inside.
///    This matches what the hardware critical section does at the CPU level.
/// 4. **SeqCst**: Sequential Consistency is the strongest ordering. It combines
///    Acquire and Release semantics AND guarantees a single total order of all
///    SeqCst operations across all threads/cores. Use this if you need the
///    strongest guarantees, but it may have performance implications.
/// 5. **Relaxed**: Only guarantees atomicity, no ordering guarantees. This is
///    why your original replacement with Relaxed was insufficient - it doesn't
///    prevent reordering with non-atomic operations outside the critical section.
/// - If channels are only accessed within critical sections: Acquire/Release is sufficient
/// - If channels might be accessed outside critical sections: You need SeqCst
/// - If unsure: Use SeqCst (it's correct, just potentially slower)
/// The critical_section::with already prevents concurrent access from the same core
/// (via interrupt disabling) and from other cores (via spinlock/mutex on RP2040),
